Kayak Kailua — The Complete Guide to Paddling Oahu's Windward Waters

Flexible (guided tours from 4–5 hours) • Kailua Beach, Oahu • moderate difficulty

Book Now — $149

Ready to Book?

Select your date and reserve your spot. Tours fill up fast — especially during peak season.

Loading booking calendar...

Quick Facts

Duration
Flexible (guided tours from 4–5 hours)
Difficulty
moderate
Location
Kailua Beach, Oahu
Price
$149 per person (guided tour)
Group Size
2–10 guests

Highlights

  • Paddle from Oahu's most beginner-friendly launch at Kailua Beach Park
  • Multiple route options: the Mokulua Islands, Flat Island, Popoia, or inside the reef
  • Protected waters inside Kailua Bay — calmer than North Shore, warmer than Hawaii Kai
  • Year-round paddling with consistent trade wind conditions
  • Sea turtles, spotted eagle rays, and seasonal whale watching

What's Included

  • Premium sit-on-top kayak (single or tandem)
  • PFD (life jacket) and paddle
  • Snorkel gear (mask, fins, snorkel)
  • Dry bag for personal items
  • Professional guide (CPR/First Aid certified)
  • Light snacks and water
  • Pre-launch paddling instruction and safety briefing

What to Bring

  • → Reef-safe sunscreen (required by Hawaii law)
  • → Swimsuit and rash guard
  • → Water shoes or secure sandals
  • → Hat and polarized sunglasses
  • → Reusable water bottle
  • → Waterproof phone case or GoPro

Frequently Asked Questions

Kailua Bay is uniquely suited for kayaking because of its protected reef, consistent but manageable trade winds, and iconic island destinations like the Mokuluas. Unlike the North Shore (which can have massive winter surf) or Hawaii Kai (which lacks the same open-ocean island destinations), Kailua offers the best balance of adventure and accessibility year-round. The bay's geography creates a natural paddling corridor that's exciting but rarely dangerous, and the view of the Ko'olau Mountains from the water is unmatched anywhere on the island.
No — that's one of the best things about Kailua. Complete beginners can paddle here safely with a guide. The bay is protected by a large barrier reef that keeps swells small, and the water is warm and shallow near shore. Our guides provide thorough instruction before launch, and 90% of first-timers feel confident within the first 15 minutes on the water. That said, if you have never kayaked and plan to go without a guide, we strongly recommend sticking inside the reef and avoiding offshore winds.
There are four main routes: (1) The Mokulua Islands — our signature 5-hour guided tour to the twin islets with snorkeling and tide pool exploration; (2) Flat Island — a shorter, easier paddle to a small island just offshore, great for families; (3) Popoia Island — a less-visited bird sanctuary island with good snorkeling; (4) Inside the Reef — a relaxed route staying inside the protective barrier reef, ideal for beginners and photographers. Your guide will choose the best route based on conditions that day.
Kailua is a year-round paddling destination, but each season has its character. Summer (May–October) brings consistent trade winds at 10–18 mph and calmer south-facing waters — this is the most reliable window. Winter (November–March) can bring larger north swells and occasional Kona winds, but also offers the chance to see humpback whales and crystal-clear water. Spring and fall are the sweet spots with mild winds, warm water, and smaller crowds. Morning tours (7–11 AM) are best year-round: glassy water, cooler temperatures, and the best light for photos.
Hawaiian green sea turtles (honu) are practically guaranteed — they feed on the seagrass beds inside the bay and often surface right next to kayaks. Spotted eagle rays glide through the shallows, especially in the morning. The coral heads inside the reef host vibrant reef fish — butterflyfish, parrotfish, triggerfish, and the occasional octopus. From December through April, humpback whales can be spotted breaching in the distance. The Mokulua Islands are also a protected seabird sanctuary with wedge-tailed shearwaters and red-footed boobies.
Both are excellent but for different reasons. Kailua has the Mokulua Islands as a destination — a real island landing experience with tide pools, seabirds, and summit views. Kaneohe Bay has the famous sandbar (Ahu o Laka), which is a unique experience at low tide. Kailua's water tends to be clearer with better snorkeling. Kaneohe's water is more protected and shallower — better for very young kids. Kailua has better facilities (parking, showers, restrooms, nearby food). If you want an island adventure with great snorkeling, choose Kailua. If you want to walk on a sandbar in the middle of the bay, choose Kaneohe.
